commentBody: I have never heard anyone refer to a recording studio as a STU.
Not too difficult for a Saturday puzzle.
What’s an oven bag?
Love seeing Bread and Roses in a puzzle, it's a beautiful song.

I've traveled on the Flying Ranee train before- some interesting trivia about this train service- around 90% of the world's diamonds are processed in Surat and trains like the Flying Ranee are used by a network of couriers called 'Angadiyas' who typically board 'second-class' compartments and blend in with everyday commuters to transport diamonds from Surat to Mumbai. Angadiyas travel in teams, often buying multiple train seats, taking turns to sleep, and carrying bags containing diamonds and cash worth hundreds of millions of $ disguised as simple, unassuming luggage. Everything is completely based on the honour system.
That felt challenging but I completed it in 2/3 of my average time. What does that mean?

So I finished this in a very fast Saturday time for me but I don’t think it was very easy—there was a ton of trivia and tangential clues that I wouldn’t ordinarily get quickly. But I had a Slum Dog Millionaire solve where I can pinpoint the stuff I did know. Thanks to my wife for introducing me to LABNEH. My son for his brief obsession with PROTEINSHAKES. Michael Connelly for his great BLOODMOON novel. BOWEN Yang who solved my first NYT crossword in a celebrity solving challenge back when that was a thing. The movie INAWORLD which I never saw but remember the trailer for (ironically). TAILGATE parties for the Brewers at Miller Park. More than enough to make inroads into the crevices of the rest of the puzzle.

So thanks Malaika and Erik for this trip down memory lane, and also for the brilliant cross of Fresh Rolls and Semi Professional, adored both those clues!
I enjoyed the puzzle, but don’t think of popcorn being in tins.

The five stages of Solving Out West on a lonely Saturday eve: Denial - "Do they expect me to know about 1910 slogans, condiments, grains, politicians, and parlor socials?" Anger - "GETME! a SNAIL would have better ATBATS on this grid!" OK, Malaika Handa and Erik Agard, looking at CHU, PLAYTIMESOVER! It's up and ATOM. You're lookin' at the PRO TEEM now. Bargaining - (NODSAT at half-empty grid). OK. Pearl-clutching . . . Priggish? Starchy? Prissy? Puritan? Genteel? Ain't it funny how you're willing to try anything when you got nothing but stacks of white squares to fill? Maybe if I knew a spell . . .Hey presto, open SESAMEOIL! Depression - "Ugh. There ain't any OVENBAGS big enough to crawl into when you ERASE more than ya fill!" Acceptance "INAWORLD where anything is possible, you OTTER be ready for anything. . .and I accept this gold star on behalf of anyone else who ran the alphabet for that one letter that got you to the happy music. And for those of you who missed the last blood moon, here's a fine ditty about our good Ole yellow orb. Sheesh, now I'm stuck on songs about the moon. Goodnight, amigos! https://youtu.be/L1Y6W1FaSVQ?si=mM0wXRlYDZhEMOtr Next BLOODMOON: 31st of Dec, 2028.

For me this was the same experience as last night.  Lotsa times I felt like I was utterly stuck, only for a stray guess to unravel (or ravel?) everything.

I also winced a bit on a few clues that I suspect wouldn't pass the weekend test by the old standards.  Was that the key gimme that I can get now that I can't get in the archives?  

Ah, the mysterious mental wanderings that can be enjoyed only by those who are overly lucky.

I would have done did this puzzle a whole lot faster had if everyone just accepted that popcorn comes in TUBS, not TINS.

(Hope you like my Eddie from Kentucky homage.  Eddie, if you're out there, I hope your coffee is as good as mine.  Hell, I hope it's better.)
For a while I thought it was BlooDANDROSES, and I thought, boy howdy, those suffragettes knew how to throw down.
Kinda getting that "talking to myself" feeling, if you know what I mean
When the jar lid won’t TWIST open SESAME OIL,  just a drop or two, often works.
This one fell together rather quickly, even though it felt like it wouldn’t.  A lot of, well, let’s try this, and it seemed to stick, or was close enough to get there.
OVENBAG and STU?? Cmon.
Joan Didion's daughter was named after 38A. It made the fill easy, but made me sad to think of her.

This puzzle was saturated with way too many proper names and other trivia to be enjoyable for me. I still almost managed to do it on my own, but the NW corner was just too much. TRE is a name?! Olympic medalist in *golf*? I love me some summer Olympic games, but *golf*? I just don't get how that's even a spectator sport. Why would anybody want to watch people walk around a lawn and occasionally swing a stick? RANEE? We're supposed to know random train names now? Ok then. Next week: iconic train from Warsaw to Zakopane!* Speaking of random, [IN A] WORLD felt incredibly so. I wanted [the] there 🤷🏽

Given all my difficulties, I'm proud of being able to resolve that heIIish corner with just two lookups (the train name and what "nuclide" means).

Elsewhere I struggled, too, but my guesses crossed well enough and they turned out to be correct, in the end.

Now, I liked much of the clueing, but all the trivia spoiled the puzzle for me.

* It's Tatry (The Tatras, a mountain range)

As a Scot I puzzled for a while over why 'whisky' didn't work for 58A. 
No-one orders a SCOTCH in Scotland. It's only ever whisky. I didn't even think of SCOTCH.

Just home from eight glorious days in Scotland, visiting my daughter and grandson in Glasgow, sampling the culinary delights of the Edinburgh dining scene, and birding in the Kingdom of Fife, this bright puzzle was harder than usual for my jet lagged brain.  I bamboozled myself into plunking down capybara instead of SWAMPRAT, which caused no end of confusion on the left side. Once I got that sorted the rest fell into place, and I enjoyed the lovely coincidence of a Scotland related clue.

The way the answers line up, it seems as if the NEOPET is riding on a NEWCAR, and that the TAILGATE doubles as a RENTPARTY.

Off topic, but Natick (the town) appeared in a Jeopardy clue last night.  Perhaps its days of obscurity are over.

A kinder, gentler EA? Perhaps Ms Handa was the moderating factor. At any rate, there were enough knowns in each quadrant to give me a foothold.

This one was a struggle for me.  NE nearly did me in.  Left it overnight, tried again just now and inch by grudging inch it came together.  An awful lot of factoids I simply didn't know, crossing with seemingly impenetrable wordplay, made for a surprise when all the squares were finally filled in and the happy music played.  Under my Saturday average to boot.  Yet, inexplicably, I didn't feel much joy at the end.  Just one of those days, I guess.

I definitely did get fooled by DO NOT enter for 3D and capybara for 15D but I realized those wouldn't work when I suspected 22A was going to be TEEM.   The middle (36 and 37A) fell pretty quickly for me after some crosses were filled in, but 32A took awhile.

For the last month, I’ve been completing the Daily Puzzle, then going to the archive and completing the puzzle for the same calendar day in 2015. They both happen to land on the same day of the week (5/30/15 is also a Saturday). 

Strangely, the May 30 puzzle from 2015 has 4 answers — ICONIC, TWISTS, OTTERS, and DON’T ERASE — that correlate with answers from today’s puzzle: 55A, 1D, 48D, 3D. 

Weird, huh? I wonder how that happens.

As an audio engineer, who also plays a good amount of baseball, I might gently take issue with two upper right clues:

No one has ever called a recording studio a “Stu.”  Like, ever in the history of recording studios :)
 
And, a person has to time up (verb) the ball during an at bat (noun). But I don’t follow the line between “times up” and “at bats.” Especially since one of those is a plural noun and the other is a third person present tense verb. A baseball specific option could be: plate appearances

2D [Flying ___ (train between Mumbai and Surat)] For a great film about another train trip in India, but this time to Calcutta, you've got to see "Lion." It is based on a real-life biography. It stars Zev Patel and Nicole Kidman. Fascinating! I listened to the audiobook and also watched the film twice. Great for techies. https://www.imdb.com/title/tt3741834/?ref_=fn_t_1

I’ve solved enough solo and collaborative puzzles by Erik Agard to say:
•	Junk in the grid will be rare or nonexistent.
•	There will be wit and humor.
•	The puns will be the good kind that make you nod with respect.
•	There will be recognition of minority groups.
•	The construction will be seamless.
•	There will be over-arching quality, the kind that makes you feel, “Dang, he’s good!”

Grateful to you, Erik!  Your 83 Times puzzles (and puzzles elsewhere) consistently showcase the art and science of crosswords. 

Malaika, your puzzles shine too – you are off to a terrific start in your nine Times creations, with your skilled grids that gleam with personality.

Today’s puzzle was rife with colorful answers, clever clues, and sweet areas of bite – and I knew it would be a highlight of my day when I saw the names atop it.  Of course, it delivered.  Thank you, Erik and Malaika!

(Help. I’m stuck in a Rio in Mexico.)

The same former mother-in-law who brought me back the genuine armadillo charango also brought back a beautiful Nutria jacket from the same trip. (I still have it here in Las Vegas, though I've gotten to wear it exactly once. It does get cold in the desert, but usually not that cold.) Strange, because we detested each other. 

It was only recently from the puzzle that I learned that armadillos can transmit leprosy. Then from Google I learned that Nutria isn't a luxury fur, but a rodent. And it was just tonight that I learned it's a SWAMP RAT. Maybe she was signaling all along. But I did ask her for the charango.
Just occurred to me that using "Quintana ___, easternmost of Mexico's 31 states" rather than "Small hopper" to clue 38A  was a real switcheROO, i.e., a TWIST.

In regard to a number of comments about the clue for STU - 320 appearances and that is the ONLY time it's ever been clued like that. Various references to 'consecutive letters of the alphabet' is one common clue - something like "R-V connector" being one clue. 

And... variations on "Apt name for a chef" is another common clue. And other than that, it's just mostly referenced to some celebrity with the nickname STU.

....

Would you believe that I had AT-A-S for "Times up" and couldn't come up with AT BATS?  It wouldn't be quite so bad if it hadn't been a clue I'd seen before.  Maybe even more than once.  But I couldn't come up with it.

I was also thrown completely off track by having AP COURSES instead of AP CLASSES.  I never questioned my answer.  And so the entire NE was ungettable.  The 3-letter recording place began with an "R".  SELMA began with an SO.  And I had "U" instead of "A" for the 2nd letter of the magazine name, giving me LUCE.  Don't ask.  What a mess.

So I resorted to cheating on BOWEN and everything fell into place after that.  Nice clue for TEAMSTERS.

I absolutely don't remember the slogan BREAD AND ROSES either for women or for labor movements.  Is it from before my time?

What's not before my time is ORLON.  What a great fabric that was!  Where did it go?  Now we have polyester, which doesn't keep you warm in the winter and doesn't keep you cool in the summer.  I hate polyester.  Thought for the day:  Wear cotton!

And are there any TREYs named TRE?  Maybe in some other country?

One of those ones you think you’re gonna nail, but don’t quite.

Re: 57A - We had the good fortune to be in Rome last fall, and during our tour of the Colosseum, our Italian guide told us how the Empire's aristocrats would buy sections of seats to accommodate both the family and their servants, who would bring in small grills and prepare food. "Ah, the origin of tailgating," we joked. Our guide looked blankly at us and asked, "What is 'tailgating'?" So we explained, and his expression went from polite amusement to disbelief. "Really? People do this for sporting events?" he asked. We just gestured to the Colosseum. He shrugged and said, "Well, we don't have that here anymore."

Okay....I'm back.  I had 3 letters circled as "possible but not sure" entries--especially in 20D....  so I solved the puzzle with no errors.

I consulted the Resident Oldie (85 year-old Vet and retired psychologist) on a couple of points.  He has never heard--or heard of--BREAD AND ROSES.   

Also (just as a "by the way") I asked for an opinion on the MYERS-Briggs; he agrees that it is over-simplified, but suggests it is a simple way to introduce folks to the concept of personality types.  He did not use it in his practice.

And finally:  I postponed entering TRE because it's perhaps a *nickname* but not a man's name.  I've know a couple of TREYs--note the spelling--which designated the guys as "the Third" (having a III after the surname)-- but they were CALLED 'TREy'....not NAMED 'TREy.'  

Okay, I guess I've picked my daily nit.  On with the day and Row 5 of the quilt I'm making...

4D[Nuclide, e.g] with the answer ATOM is a classic, and I do mean Classic, example of the oft-cited dictum that a crossword is not a dictionary with precise definitions.  Also of "Too Close" syndrome.

When one talks of the "nuclide", one is focussing on the behavior of the nucleus of an atom--how many protons, how many neutrons, etc.  "Atom" is used primarily when ignoring the nucleus.  Almost everyone who uses "atom" is referring to the chemical nature of the element.  In this discussion, the nucleus is merely a dimensionless point charge which holds the electrons in place.

Sure, there are phrases like "splitting the atom", but these are terms used more in sci fi than in serious discussions.

But that's OK!!!

Crosswords are all about lateral thinking, about fuzzy, imperfect logic.

Tough one to crack into and flowed nicely once I did! There was some arcana like ROO and RANEE I was unfamiliar with but the crosses made them manageable. ICONIC puzzle, took me 16:03 to get the Gold Star.

Hey,
I’ve been a subscriber for a few years now, I thoroughly enjoy the crossword and have recognized so many repeat clues with the same answer over consecutive days that I’m starting to improve my average times. One thing I have also noticed is that some clues either don’t make sense for any of us outside the US and that’s perfectly fine, I still get it in the end or its just the constructor making up hogwash to fit the grid. 

This one in particular stands out and I’m starting to question the fact checking and if there even is any. 
Saturday CW, 5/30/2026, 1A.  Tre!! 

In what parallel Italian universe is the word for number three ever used as a man’s name?   
As an Italian, I have to ask “🤌🏾”? Never has this name appeared, it’s not common and who is determining that it ever was?
Even if one person named their kid, Tre, would it even be common enough to be considered as a crossword entry for an “Italian” name? 
Please explain. 

Arrivederci,

I’ll add that technically, there’s still a bit of a misalignment there. [Times up] is really the stat known as plate appearances, which is the total of all the times a player has taken his turn at bat. But certain outcomes are not figured into AT BATS: walks, hit-by-pitches, catcher’s interference. So you don’t know whether a “time up” is a AT BAT until it’s completed. 

This is deep into the weeds, and the clue is close enough for crosswords.

below a bit more for context.
=======
India has a system of express trains operated by Indian Railways and overseen by the Ministry of Railways of the Government of India. As of 2023, it maintains over 108,706 km (67,547 mi) of tracks, spanning across 68,584 km (42,616 mi) in route length, and operates nearly 3,000 express trains daily. According to the Ministry of Railways, express trains travel faster and have limited stops than ordinary passenger trains. Any passenger train with an average speed higher than 55 km/h (34 mph) is considered super-fast.

@Lynn

Right.  Here's an example.  The element cobalt has many nuclides, but only one is stable.  That nuclide has 27 protons and 32 neutrons.  The Co-60 nuclide has one more neutron (27 protons, 33 neutrons), and is unstable and therefore radioactive.

None of that would make sense if I just said "cobalt atom" instead of "cobalt nuclide".  The "cobalt atom" designation generally includes a mixture of  the the common nuclides, whether stable or radioactive.

Now I could say the cobalt-59 atom or the cobalt-60 atom, but that would sound odd, I think, to someone in the field.

Still, the bottom line is it's ok?  It's a puzzle.

@Francis

I have long since forgotten most of what I learned in PChem as a biochemistry major who went on to an MD. But as you probably know, in medicine, specifically radiology and radiation therapy, "radioNUCLIDES" are frequently used because they are atoms that are unstable. When they decay they emit useful energy that can penetrate tissue to generate images or kill cancer cells.

@Mtmetz

Yes, that's right!  For example, Iodine-131 is used in thyroid studies.  

Iodine also has only one stable "nuclide" or "isotope", and that is I-127, which has 53 protons and 74 neutrons.  But I-131, which has four more (78) neutrons, has the special *nuclear* property of being a beta emitter, and therefore detected by machine that can detect radiation.

Iodine-127 would be useless for this because it is not radioactive, and is therefore not "loud" for our radiation detectors.

But, and this is key, all isotopes of Iodine, 127, 131 and all the rest have *exactly* the same chemistry.  So the body uses all iodine *atoms* identically.  And so we can be sure the biochemistry of I-131 is exactly the same as I-127.  And if I-131 is being concentrated in one part of the body, we can be sure that *all* iodine is being concentrated in that part of the body.
